name: Tarasque
description: The Tarasque is a legendary creature from French folklore, primarily associated with the town of Tarascon in Provence. Described as a fearsome dragon-like beast, it possessed a lion's head, six short legs resembling a bear's paws, an ox-like body covered with a turtle shell, and a scorpion-like tail. Its origins are often linked to Saint Martha, who is said to have tamed the beast with holy water and prayer. The Tarasque was a symbol of destruction and terror, ravaging the countryside and preying on humans and animals alike. Its defeat by Saint Martha is celebrated annually in Tarascon with a festival, where an effigy of the Tarasque is paraded through the streets, representing the triumph of good over evil and the power of faith.
subtitle: A dragon-like beast tamed by Saint Martha.
appearance: A chimera-like creature with the head of a lion, the body of an ox covered with a turtle shell, six bear-like legs, and a venomous, scorpion-like tail.
habitat: Marshes and forests near Tarascon, France.
behavior: Aggressive and territorial, the Tarasque was known for its destructive rampages, preying on both humans and animals.

keyMyths.title: Saint Martha and the Tarasque
keyMyths.description: The most famous story tells of Saint Martha arriving in Tarascon and confronting the fearsome Tarasque. Armed with only holy water and unwavering faith, she approached the beast and sprinkled it with holy water, causing it to become docile. The townspeople, who had long suffered under the Tarasque's reign of terror, then dispatched the weakened creature. This tale highlights the power of faith and the triumph of good over evil.
keyMyths.source: The Golden Legend, by Jacobus de Voragine

keyMyths.title: The Origins of Tarascon
keyMyths.description: The legend of the Tarasque is inextricably linked to the founding of the town of Tarascon. The town's name is believed to be derived from the creature itself. The story reinforces the town's identity and provides a historical narrative centered on the defeat of a monstrous threat, attributing the town's salvation to the intervention of Saint Martha.

keyMyths.title: The Tarasque Festival
keyMyths.description: Each year, the town of Tarascon celebrates the victory over the Tarasque with a festival. A large effigy of the creature is paraded through the streets, often accompanied by music, dancing, and theatrical performances. This festival serves as a reminder of the town's history and the enduring power of the legend. It is a vibrant expression of local culture and identity.
keyMyths.source: Observations of the Tarasque Festival in Tarascon

sources.author: Jacobus de Voragine
sources.description: A collection of hagiographies, including the story of Saint Martha and the Tarasque.
sources.text: A collection of hagiographies, including the story of Saint Martha and the Tarasque.

sources.author: Jean-Claude Schmitt
sources.work: Ghosts in the Middle Ages: The Living Dead and the Afterlife
sources.description: Provides context on the role of monsters and saints in medieval folklore.
sources.text: Provides context on the role of monsters and saints in medieval folklore.

sources.author: Ronald Hutton
sources.work: The Stations of the Sun: A History of the Ritual Year in Britain
sources.description: Offers insight into the function and importance of festivals and their connection to local mythology.
sources.text: Offers insight into the function and importance of festivals and their connection to local mythology.
